ranges.label: No Trauma Exposure
ranges.description: You did not report experiencing any of the 17 potentially traumatic events listed. This screening result is favorable, though it does not assess all possible life stressors. If you have other concerns, professional support remains available.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your current well-being and coping strategies
Stay informed about healthy stress management techniques for the future
If you have other mental health concerns not covered by this checklist, professional help is still available

ranges.label: Low Trauma Exposure
ranges.description: You reported exposure to a small number of potentially traumatic events. Even limited trauma exposure can sometimes lead to lasting psychological effects. Monitoring your well-being and seeking help if distressing symptoms arise is advisable.
suggestions: Monitor yourself for trauma-related symptoms such as intrusive memories, avoidance, or hypervigilance
Practice healthy coping strategies including regular exercise, adequate sleep, and social connection
If these events continue to bother you or cause nightmares or flashbacks, consider speaking with a mental health professional
The PCL-5 (PTSD Checklist) can help assess whether you are experiencing post-traumatic stress symptoms

ranges.label: Moderate Trauma Exposure
ranges.description: You reported exposure to several potentially traumatic events. Multiple trauma exposures increase the risk of developing PTSD and other stress-related conditions. A follow-up mental health assessment is recommended to evaluate your well-being.
suggestions: Consider completing the PCL-5 to screen for PTSD symptoms related to your trauma exposure
A professional mental health assessment can help determine whether you would benefit from treatment
Trauma-focused therapies such as CPT or EMDR are effective evidence-based treatments if symptoms are present
Maintain supportive social connections, as social support is a strong protective factor after trauma
Avoid using alcohol or substances as a way to cope with distressing memories

ranges.label: High Trauma Exposure
ranges.description: You reported exposure to many potentially traumatic events, indicating a high cumulative trauma burden. Cumulative trauma significantly increases the risk of PTSD, depression, and anxiety. A comprehensive mental health evaluation is recommended.
suggestions: A comprehensive trauma-informed mental health assessment is strongly recommended as soon as possible
Connect with a therapist who specializes in trauma treatment, such as CPT, PE, or EMDR
If you are experiencing crisis-level distress, contact the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline
Build and maintain a safety plan that includes trusted people and coping strategies
Remember that seeking help is a sign of strength, and effective treatments for trauma-related conditions are available

ranges.label: Normal
ranges.description: Your psychological distress level is within the normal range, suggesting you are coping well with everyday challenges. Most people in the general population score here. Continue nurturing habits and relationships that support your well-being.
suggestions: Continue maintaining the healthy habits and routines that support your well-being
Stay socially connected and engaged in activities that bring you meaning and enjoyment
Be aware that mental health can fluctuate, and seeking support early during difficult periods is beneficial

ranges.label: Mild Distress
ranges.description: You are experiencing mild psychological distress that may be affecting your mood, energy, or daily functioning. This is common during stressful life periods and often responds to self-care. If symptoms persist, consider professional consultation.
suggestions: Pay attention to emotional changes and identify the specific stressors contributing to your distress
Practice evidence-based stress management techniques such as mindfulness, progressive muscle relaxation, or deep breathing
Maintain regular physical exercise, which has strong evidence for improving mood and reducing anxiety
Consider seeking professional help if your distress persists or begins to interfere with daily responsibilities

ranges.label: Moderate Distress
ranges.description: Your psychological distress is elevated and likely affecting work, relationships, and daily functioning. This score falls in the clinical range seen in people seeking mental health services. Professional support can help develop coping strategies.
suggestions: Seeking counseling or psychotherapy is recommended at this level of distress
Share your feelings with a trusted friend, family member, or colleague for emotional support
Maintain regular routines including consistent sleep, meals, and physical activity
Avoid relying on alcohol, substances, or other unhealthy coping mechanisms to manage your distress
Consider whether workplace or relationship issues may be contributing factors that need to be addressed

ranges.label: Severe Distress
ranges.description: Your psychological distress is very high and likely impairing daily functioning, work, and relationships. This level of distress typically warrants prompt professional intervention. Please prioritize seeking help, as effective treatments exist.
suggestions: Please seek professional mental health help as soon as possible; this level of distress is treatable
If you are having thoughts of self-harm or suicide, contact the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline immediately
Ensure you have trusted people around you who can provide support during this difficult time
Seeking help is a sign of strength, not weakness, and effective treatments such as therapy and medication are available
Focus on basic self-care including sleep, nutrition, and safety while you arrange professional support

ranges.label: Few Hypomanic Features
ranges.description: You reported few hypomanic symptoms, scoring well below the clinical screening threshold of 14. Periods of elevated mood or increased activity have not been a prominent feature of your experience. No particular concern is indicated at this time.
suggestions: No particular concern is indicated at this time based on your screening results
If you experience significant mood fluctuations in the future, consider re-taking this screening
Being aware of your baseline mood patterns can help you recognize meaningful changes early

ranges.label: Some Hypomanic Features
ranges.description: You reported some hypomanic symptoms but scored below the screening threshold of 14. While not screen-positive, this indicates features worth monitoring. Tracking mood patterns over time can help you and your clinician identify meaningful changes.
suggestions: Pay attention to the pattern and duration of mood fluctuations, especially periods of unusually high energy
Keeping a daily mood diary can help you identify cycles and triggers for mood changes
If mood swings begin to affect your work, relationships, or sleep, consider consulting a mental health professional
Learn about the difference between normal mood variation and clinically significant hypomania

ranges.label: Screen Positive
ranges.description: Your score meets or exceeds the screening threshold of 14, suggesting possible bipolar spectrum features. This is a screening result, not a diagnosis. A comprehensive assessment by a psychiatrist is needed to determine if bipolar disorder is present.
suggestions: Consult a psychiatrist or clinical psychologist for a comprehensive diagnostic evaluation
Keep detailed records of your mood patterns, sleep changes, and energy levels to share with your clinician
Learn about bipolar spectrum disorders from reputable sources to understand what this screening result means
Do not self-diagnose or self-medicate based on a screening score alone; professional assessment is essential
If you are currently taking antidepressants, inform your prescriber about this screening result

ranges.label: Prominent Hypomanic Features
ranges.description: You reported many hypomanic symptoms, scoring well above the clinical screening threshold. This strongly suggests bipolar spectrum features requiring professional evaluation. A comprehensive psychiatric assessment is recommended to guide treatment.
suggestions: Strongly recommended to schedule an appointment with a psychiatrist as soon as possible
Document your detailed mood history, including periods of both elevation and depression, for your clinician
Provide your doctor with a complete history of all mood episodes and their impact on your functioning
If you are currently on medication, do not adjust dosage or stop taking it without medical guidance

ranges.label: Minimal Panic Symptoms
ranges.description: Your score suggests minimal or no significant panic disorder symptoms over the past week. Panic attacks may be absent or very infrequent with little associated distress or avoidance. Continue maintaining healthy coping strategies that work for you.
suggestions: Continue maintaining healthy coping strategies and stress management practices
Practice regular relaxation techniques such as deep breathing or progressive muscle relaxation as preventive measures
If occasional anxiety arises, remind yourself that panic attacks, while distressing, are not medically dangerous

ranges.label: Mild to Moderate Panic
ranges.description: Your score indicates mild to moderate panic disorder symptoms, including some panic attacks, anticipatory anxiety, and avoidance. While not severely disabling, these symptoms may be causing meaningful distress and limiting certain activities.
suggestions: Learn about the cognitive model of panic: panic attacks are caused by catastrophic misinterpretation of bodily sensations, not actual danger
Practice diaphragmatic breathing and interoceptive exposure exercises to reduce fear of physical sensations
Consider cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), which is the gold-standard treatment for panic disorder
Keep a panic diary to identify triggers, early warning signs, and patterns in your panic attacks

ranges.label: Moderate to Severe Panic
ranges.description: Your score indicates moderate to severe panic disorder symptoms that are likely impacting your daily life, work, and social activities. Avoidance behaviors may be restricting your activities and anticipatory anxiety may be causing persistent worry.
suggestions: Professional treatment is strongly recommended; CBT with exposure therapy is highly effective for panic disorder
Discuss medication options with a psychiatrist, as SSRIs and SNRIs can significantly reduce panic symptoms
Gradually challenge avoidance behaviors with professional guidance, as avoidance maintains the panic cycle
Build a support network of trusted people who understand your condition and can provide encouragement
Avoid relying on safety behaviors or substances such as alcohol or benzodiazepines to cope with panic

ranges.label: Severe Panic
ranges.description: Your score indicates severe panic disorder symptoms with significant functional impairment across multiple life domains. You may experience frequent panic attacks, intense anticipatory anxiety, and extensive avoidance. Prompt treatment is essential.
suggestions: Please seek professional help as soon as possible; severe panic disorder is highly treatable with proper intervention
If you feel you are in crisis or unable to cope, contact the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline for immediate support
A combination of CBT and medication (such as SSRIs) is often the most effective approach for severe panic disorder
Do not avoid situations out of fear; avoidance reinforces the panic cycle and leads to increasing restriction of activities
Remember that panic disorder has among the highest treatment success rates of any mental health condition
